name: Huma Bird
description: The Huma bird, also known as the bird of paradise, is a legendary creature in Persian mythology. It is said to never alight on the ground, instead spending its entire life flying high above the earth. Some accounts claim it has no legs. The Huma is considered a symbol of happiness, good fortune, and royalty. It is believed that if the Huma's shadow falls upon someone, that person will become a king or queen. Because it never lands, the Huma is said to reproduce by laying eggs in mid-air, which hatch when they fall to the ground. Others believe the eggs hatch on the Huma's back while flying. Its diet is believed to consist only of the bones of other creatures, and it is considered a benevolent and compassionate being. The Huma bird is often depicted in Persian art and literature as a majestic bird with beautiful plumage, representing divine grace and blessings. Its existence is purely mythical, symbolizing the pursuit of unattainable ideals and the ephemeral nature of power and happiness. The bird is also associated with Sufi mysticism, representing the soul's yearning for union with the divine. In some interpretations, the Huma's shadow falling on someone represents divine favor or a spiritual awakening.
subtitle: Bird of Paradise and Good Fortune
appearance: A majestic bird with incredibly beautiful and vibrant plumage. Often depicted with golden or multicolored feathers. Some accounts describe it as having no legs, while others portray it with strong, elegant wings and a long, graceful tail.
habitat: The skies above the world, constantly flying at great heights. Never lands on earth.
behavior: Constantly flying, never landing. Benevolent and compassionate, bringing good fortune and royalty. Associated with spiritual yearning and divine grace.
weaknesses: Its shadow can bring unwanted attention or political intrigue.
Vulnerable if ever forced to land.

keyMyths.title: The Shadow of Kings
keyMyths.description: It is said that the shadow of the Huma Bird falling upon someone will bestow upon them the right to rule, making them a king or queen. This myth emphasizes the bird's association with royalty and good fortune.

sources.author: Firdausi
sources.description: A foundational text of Persian literature that may allude to the symbolism of the Huma Bird.
sources.text: A foundational text of Persian literature that may allude to the symbolism of the Huma Bird.

sources.author: Annemarie Schimmel
sources.description: Discusses the Huma Bird's association with Sufi mysticism and the soul's journey.
